saveframes.name: sample_1
tags:
Filamentous TasA fibrallized through addition of TapA at pH 7.0, 150 mM NaCl and subsequent ultracentrifugation for 17 h at 130000 x g. 
TasA is isotopically labeled and perdeuterated (2H, 13C, 15N) and 100 % back-exchanged in aqueous buffer. 
TapA is unlabeled and only present in negligible concentration according to Western blot analysis.
Buffer exchanged prior to NMR measurement, data acquired in 1.3 mm rotor.

saveframes.name: sample_2
tags: Filamentous TasA fibrallized through slow concentration (2-3 days) on a Amicon stirring device equipped with a 10 kDa membrane made of regenerated cellulose.

saveframes.name: sample_3
tags:
Filamentous TasA fibrallized through slow concentration (2-3 days) on a Amicon stirring device equipped with a 10 kDa membrane made of regenerated cellulose. 
Expression with 2-13C glycerol as carbon source.

saveframes.name: sample_4
tags:
Filamentous TasA fibrallized through slow concentration (2-3 days) on a Amicon stirring device equipped with a 10 kDa membrane made of regenerated cellulose. 
Expression with 1,3-13C glycerol as carbon source.

saveframes.name: sample_conditions_1
tags: Buffer conditions during all measurements were set to 20 mM PO4 pH 7, 50 mM NaCl

saveframes.name: software_1
tags: The CCPN NMR assignment and data analysis application

saveframes.name: assigned_chemical_shifts_1
tags:
Assignments derived from a set of spectra recorded with the Bruker provided pulse sequence published in 
'Rapid Proton-Detected NMR Assignment for Proteins with Fast Magic Angle Spinning', 
Journal of the American Chemical Society 2014 136 (35), 12489-12497 (doi: 10.1021/ja507382j).

saveframes.name: assigned_chemical_shifts_2
tags:
Assignments of the protonated protein derived from CC spectra with different labeling and mixing time. 
Results are based on the deuterated assignments which were isotope-corrected and matched to most probable match.

saveframes.name: assigned_chemical_shifts_3
tags: Residues already contained in assigned_chemical_shifts_1 that show a second set of signals. (Peak doubling)
